paragraphs: Amber Jewel is an Australian-bred Japanese-type plum (Prunus salicina) that began as a chance seedling found in 1978 in the garden of R. A. Yates at Capel River, Western Australia. It was originally named Teak Gold, patented under that name, then renamed Amber Jewel for market. The fruit is large and conical with red skin over a cream to yellow background.

Its standout feature is amber to golden-yellow flesh that is very sweet, meaty, and crisp with low acidity. Amber Jewel matures late, about a month after Santa Rosa, and is partially self-fertile, pairing well with pollinators such as Santa Rosa or Laroda. It also has notably good storage and shelf life.

claims.text: Australian-bred Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) that began as a chance seedling found in 1978 by R. A. Yates at Capel River, Western Australia, originally named Teak Gold
Large conical fruit with red skin over a cream to yellow background
Amber to golden-yellow flesh that is very sweet, meaty, and crisp with low acidity
Matures late, about a month after Santa Rosa, and is partially self-fertile with pollinators such as Santa Rosa or Laroda
Notably good storage and shelf life

sources.name: US Plant Patent PP10277: Plum tree Teak Gold

paragraphs: Angelina is the late-season Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) widely marketed as Angeleno or Angelino. It was released in the United States in 1995 by Sunworld International and has become one of the dominant very late plums in commercial markets worldwide.

The skin colours from green to a dark, almost black purple when ripe, over firm pale-yellow to amber flesh. The flavour is sweet and well balanced, with dense, crunchy flesh rather than soft and juicy. It ripens very late and stores and ships exceptionally well, keeping firm in the bowl for a long time.

claims.text: Late-season Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) marketed as Angeleno or Angelino, released in the United States in 1995 by Sunworld International
Skin ripens from green to dark, almost black purple, over firm pale-yellow to amber flesh
Sweet, well-balanced flavour with dense, firm, crunchy flesh rather than soft and juicy
Ripens very late and stores and ships exceptionally well

sources.name: Tough Kraut: Prunus salicina 'Angeleno' care guide

paragraphs: Angelina Burdett is a heritage English dessert plum (Prunus domestica), raised by Mr Dowling of Woolston, Southampton, in about 1850 and first described in 1853. It is a completely separate variety from the modern Angeleno (Angelina) plum.

The fruit is medium sized and round-oval with purple to blue-black skin carrying a heavy bloom, over firm, juicy, yellowish-green flesh with good flavour. It is grown mainly as a dessert plum, has a semi-freestone stone, hangs and keeps well after picking, and ripens from late August into early September.

claims.text: Heritage English dessert plum (Prunus domestica) raised by Mr Dowling of Woolston, Southampton, about 1850 and first described in 1853
Medium round-oval fruit with purple to blue-black bloomed skin over firm, juicy, yellowish-green flesh
Grown mainly as a dessert plum
Semi-freestone, hangs and keeps well after picking, ripening late August into early September

paragraphs: Coe's Golden Drop is a heritage English European plum (Prunus domestica), raised by Jervaise Coe in Suffolk at the end of the 18th century, said to be a cross between Green Gage and White Magnum Bonum. It is one of the historic European dessert plums and considered among the best of the late-season varieties.

The fruit has golden yellow skin, often flecked with reddish brown and carrying a cinnamon-like bloom, over tender, juicy golden flesh. The flavour is rich and sweet with a distinctive apricot character, balanced by some acidity from its Magnum parent. It is a large freestone plum that ripens late, around mid-September, and needs a pollinator such as Green Gage or D'Agen to set fruit.

claims.text: Heritage English European plum (Prunus domestica) raised by Jervaise Coe in Suffolk at the end of the 18th century, reputedly Green Gage crossed with White Magnum Bonum
A historic European dessert plum, regarded as one of the best late-season varieties
Golden yellow skin with reddish brown flecks and a cinnamon bloom over tender golden flesh
Rich, sweet flavour with a distinctive apricot character, balanced by some acidity
Large freestone fruit that ripens late, around mid-September
Needs a pollinator such as Green Gage or D'Agen to set fruit

sources.name: Gardening Know How: Coe's Golden Drop Gage

paragraphs: The D'Agen prune (Prune d'Agen, also called Petite d'Agen, Ente or the California French prune) is a European plum (Prunus domestica) originating in the Agen region of southwestern France, the classic plum grown for drying into prunes. In California it is known simply as the French prune, and what Europeans call d'Agen is the same variety.

It is a small to medium purple to blue-skinned plum with firm, sweet, yellow-green flesh, prized for its dense flesh and high sugar that concentrate beautifully when dried. The variety was introduced to California by Louis Pellier in the 1850s, founding the Californian prune industry. It is best known for drying and prune making rather than fresh dessert use.

claims.text: European plum (Prunus domestica) from the Agen region of southwestern France, the classic drying/prune plum
Also called Petite d'Agen, Ente, and the California French prune; the French prune in California equals the d'Agen of Europe
Small to medium plum with purple to blue skin and firm, sweet yellow-green flesh
Grown chiefly for drying into prunes, with flesh and sugar that concentrate well when dried
Introduced to California by Louis Pellier in the 1850s, founding the Californian prune industry

sources.name: Taste of France: What exactly is an Agen prune?

paragraphs: The Damson is a small European plum (Prunus domestica subsp. insititia) whose name derives from the Latin for plum of Damascus. It is a traditional cooking plum long grown in Britain, with skin ranging from dark blue through indigo to near black over yellow-green flesh, and a typically ovoid shape pointed at one end.

Damsons are distinctive for being both high in sugar and highly astringent, giving a rich flavour that is usually considered too tart for fresh eating. Most are clingstone, with the flesh adhering to the stone. They are grown mainly for cooking and preserving, going into jam, fruit preserves, and damson gin, and ripen in late summer to autumn.

claims.text: Small European plum, Prunus domestica subsp. insititia, named from the Latin for plum of Damascus
Dark blue to near-black skin over yellow-green flesh, ovoid and pointed at one end
Both high in sugar and highly astringent, with a rich tart flavour usually too tart for fresh eating
Most are clingstone, with flesh adhering to the stone
Grown mainly for cooking and preserving, including jam and damson gin

paragraphs: Elephant Heart is a large Japanese-type plum (Prunus salicina) raised by Luther Burbank in California in the early 1920s and released after his death. The fruit is big and heart-shaped with thick bronze-green skin that turns reddish-purple when fully ripe.

It is best known for its juicy, blood-red, freestone flesh and rich, sweet, distinctive flavour, which is why it is grouped among the blood plums. Elephant Heart ripens very late in the season, one of the last plums to mature, and crops better with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa. It is excellent fresh and also used for canning and freezing.

claims.text: Large Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) raised by Luther Burbank in California in the early 1920s and released after his death
Large heart-shaped fruit with thick bronze-green skin turning reddish-purple when ripe
Juicy blood-red freestone flesh with a rich sweet distinctive flavour
Ripens very late, among the last plums to mature, and crops better with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa

sources.name: Trees of Antiquity: Elephant Heart Plum

paragraphs: Flavour Supreme (sold as Flavor Supreme) is a pluot, an interspecific plum and apricot hybrid developed by Floyd Zaiger of Zaiger Genetics in California. Dave Wilson Nursery, the introducer of Zaiger's interspecific fruit, records it as the first pluot Zaiger released, in 1988.

The fruit has mottled green skin with red speckling, an unassuming look that hides intensely sweet, juicy, red (Bordeaux) flesh. It is one of the earlier pluots to ripen and is not self-fertile, so it needs a polleniser such as another pluot or a Japanese plum nearby to set a crop.

claims.text: A pluot (interspecific plum and apricot hybrid) developed by Floyd Zaiger of Zaiger Genetics
Introduced by Dave Wilson Nursery as the first pluot Zaiger released, in 1988
Mottled green skin with red speckling over juicy red (Bordeaux) flesh
Intensely sweet, rich, juicy flesh
Not self-fertile, requires a polleniser such as another pluot variety

sources.name: Dave Wilson Nursery: Flavor Supreme Pluot

paragraphs: Green Gage is a European plum (Prunus domestica), the type known in France as Reine Claude after Queen Claude, wife of Francis I. The English name honours the Gage family, credited with introducing it to England in the early 18th century. The fruit is small and round-oval with green to yellowish-green skin and smooth pale green flesh.

Gages are prized as one of the finest dessert plums, with a rich, sweet, confectionery flavour, though they are also eaten unripe when they are crisp and pleasantly tart. Green Gage ripens in late summer and is not self-fertile, so it needs a compatible plum, gage, or damson nearby to pollinate it. It is eaten fresh and also stewed or made into jam.

claims.text: European plum (Prunus domestica), known in France as Reine Claude after Queen Claude, with the English name honouring the Gage family
Small round-oval fruit with green to yellowish skin and smooth pale green flesh
Among the finest dessert plums, with a rich sweet confectionery flavour, also eaten unripe when tart
Not self-fertile, needing another plum, gage, or damson as a pollinator
Eaten fresh and also stewed or made into jam

paragraphs: Gulf Beauty (Gulfbeauty) is a low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) released by the University of Florida in 1998, part of the Gulf series bred by Wayne Sherman for mild-winter, subtropical regions with high disease pressure. All UF plum cultivars carry the Gulf prefix.

It is one of the earliest ripening plums in the series, with dark reddish purple skin over yellow flesh tinged green, sweet and sub-acid when ripe (the skin itself is sour). It is noted as very resistant to bacterial canker and moderately resistant to plum leaf scald, and benefits from a Gulf series pollinator.

claims.text: Low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) released by the University of Florida in 1998, part of the Gulf series bred by Wayne Sherman
Bred for mild-winter, subtropical regions; low-chill
One of the earliest ripening plums of the series
Dark reddish purple skin over yellow flesh with a green hue
Sweet and sub-acid flesh, firm when ripe, with a sour skin
Very resistant to bacterial canker and moderately resistant to plum leaf scald

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS HS250: Growing Plums in Florida
UF/IFAS News: Four New Plum Varieties Developed By UF Researcher

paragraphs: Gulf Blaze (Gulfblaze) is a low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) bred and patented by the University of Florida as part of the Gulf series, developed by Wayne Sherman for warm, subtropical climates with high disease pressure.

The fruit has dark red to purple skin over orange flesh that is sweet and sub-acid. It ripens in the middle of the Florida plum season, roughly 8 to 14 days after Gulf Beauty. The flesh is semi-freestone, weakly attached to the pit when ripe, and the fruit is firm and round.

claims.text: Low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) bred and patented by the University of Florida Gulf series, developed by Wayne Sherman
Bred for warm, subtropical climates; low-chill
Dark red to purple skin over orange flesh
Flesh is sweet and sub-acid
Ripens mid-season, about 8 to 14 days after Gulf Beauty
Semi-freestone, with flesh weakly attached to the pit when ripe

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS HS250: Growing Plums in Florida
UF/IFAS News: Four New Plum Varieties Developed By UF Researcher

paragraphs: Gulf Gold (Gulfgold) is a low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) from the University of Florida stone fruit breeding program, part of the Gulf series bred for mild-winter, subtropical climates. Unlike the patented members of the series, it is not patented.

It is a yellow-skinned plum, often with a red blush, over yellow flesh that turns semi-soft when ripe, and is regarded as the sweetest of the Gulf series. It ripens late in the series, from late May into mid-June. A drawback is its susceptibility to plum leaf scald, which generally limits tree life in Florida.

claims.text: Low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) from the University of Florida Gulf series; not patented
Bred for mild-winter, subtropical climates; low-chill
Yellow skin with a red blush over yellow, semi-soft flesh
Sweetest of the Gulf series
Ripens late in the series, late May to mid-June
Susceptible to plum leaf scald, which generally limits tree life in Florida

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS HS250: Growing Plums in Florida
UF/IFAS News: Four New Plum Varieties Developed By UF Researcher

paragraphs: Gulf Ruby (Gulfruby) is a low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) from the University of Florida stone fruit breeding program led by Wayne Sherman, part of the Gulf series bred for warm, subtropical climates with high disease pressure. It was selected and propagated in the early 1980s and, unlike most of the series, is not patented and may be freely propagated.

The fruit has red to purple skin over sweet yellow flesh with a greenish tinge. It ripens early in the Florida plum season, roughly 7 to 10 days before Gulf Blaze. A noted quirk is that Gulf Ruby has more trouble with bacterial canker than other low-chill cultivars, which can shorten tree life.

claims.text: Low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) from the University of Florida Gulf series, bred by Wayne Sherman; selected/propagated in the early 1980s and not patented
Low-chill cultivar bred for warm, subtropical climates
Red to purple skin over sweet yellow flesh with a greenish tinge
Ripens early in the Florida plum season, about 7 to 10 days before Gulf Blaze
More prone to bacterial canker than other low-chill cultivars

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS HS250: Growing Plums in Florida
UF/IFAS News: Four New Plum Varieties Developed By UF Researcher

paragraphs: Mariposa is a Japanese-type blood plum that originated as a seedling at Pasadena, California, around 1923. It is sometimes sold as Improved Satsuma.

The fruit has deep red, juicy flesh and a rich, sweet, aromatic flavour with a tart skin, and the stone separates almost cleanly (nearly freestone). It ripens mid season (late summer in Australia). Mariposa crops best with a compatible pollinator such as Santa Rosa or Satsuma.

claims.text: Japanese-type blood plum originating as a seedling at Pasadena, California, around 1923
A Japanese blood plum, also called Improved Satsuma
Deep red juicy flesh, rich sweet aromatic flavour, nearly freestone
Ripens mid season
Best cropped with a compatible pollinator such as Santa Rosa or Satsuma

paragraphs: Narrabeen is an Australian Japanese-type plum (Prunus salicina) that arose as a chance seedling in the Narrabeen area near Sydney. It produces large, round fruit with red skin over a yellow background.

The flesh is yellow to cream, sweet, firm, and juicy, and the stone separates cleanly, making it a freestone variety. Narrabeen ripens mid season (around January to February in Australia) and crops best with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa, Satsuma, or Mariposa. It is good eaten fresh and also stewed, bottled, or made into jam.

claims.text: Australian Japanese-type plum (Prunus salicina) that arose as a chance seedling in the Narrabeen area near Sydney
Large round fruit with red skin over a yellow background
Yellow to cream flesh that is sweet, firm, and juicy, and freestone
Ripens mid season and crops best with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa, Satsuma, or Mariposa
Good fresh and also stewed, bottled, or made into jam

sources.name: Heritage Fruit Trees: Narrabeen Japanese Plum

paragraphs: President is a European plum (Prunus domestica) raised by the Rivers Nursery at Sawbridgeworth, Hertfordshire, England, and introduced around 1901. It was valued as one of the latest plums to ripen, providing fresh fruit when most other plums had finished.

The fruit is very large and oval with dark blue to purple-black skin and greenish-yellow flesh, and it suits both eating fresh and culinary use such as jam and preserves. It ripens late, in mid September in the UK. President is not self-fertile, so it needs another plum variety nearby for pollination.

claims.text: European plum (Prunus domestica) raised by the Rivers Nursery, Sawbridgeworth, Hertfordshire, introduced around 1901
Very large oval fruit with dark blue to purple-black skin and greenish-yellow flesh
Used both as a dessert plum and for culinary purposes such as preserves
A very late-ripening plum, ripening in mid September in the UK
Not self-fertile, requires another plum variety nearby for pollination

paragraphs: Ruby Blood is a Japanese-type blood plum (Prunus salicina) grown widely in Australia. The fruit is medium to large and round to heart-shaped, with dark red mottled skin over deep red, juicy flesh, which is what marks it out as a blood plum.

The flesh is sweet and the stone separates cleanly, making it a freestone variety. Ruby Blood ripens late in the season (late summer into autumn in Australia) and needs a cross-pollinator such as Narrabeen, Mariposa, or Satsuma. It is used fresh and also for preserves and jam.

claims.text: Japanese-type blood plum (Prunus salicina) grown widely in Australia
Medium to large round to heart-shaped fruit with dark red mottled skin over deep red juicy flesh
Sweet flesh, freestone with the stone separating cleanly
Ripens late in the season and needs a cross-pollinator such as Narrabeen, Mariposa, or Satsuma
Used fresh and also for preserves and jam

sources.name: Blerick Tree Farm: Plum Ruby Blood
Heritage Fruit Trees: Ruby Japanese Blood Plum

paragraphs: Santa Rosa is a Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) bred by Luther Burbank and introduced in 1906 from his nursery at Santa Rosa, California. It became Burbank's most celebrated plum and was once the dominant commercial plum in California.

The fruit has purple-red skin over amber to yellow flesh, with a sweet, slightly tart flavour. It ripens mid season. Santa Rosa is self-fertile and is widely used as a pollinator for other Japanese plums, though crops improve when grown near a second variety.

claims.text: Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) bred by Luther Burbank, introduced 1906 from Santa Rosa, California
Purple-red skin over yellow to amber flesh, sweet and slightly tart
Ripens mid season
Self-fertile and a widely used pollinator for Japanese plums

sources.name: Trees of Antiquity: Santa Rosa Plum

paragraphs: Satsuma is a Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) introduced to the United States by Luther Burbank in the late 1800s and named after the Satsuma province of Japan. It was an early and influential blood plum in commercial cultivation.

It is known as a blood plum for its dark red skin and deep red flesh, with sweet, juicy fruit. It ripens mid season. Satsuma is at most partially self-fertile and crops far better with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa or Shiro.

claims.text: Japanese plum introduced to the US by Luther Burbank in the late 1800s, named after the Satsuma province of Japan
A blood plum with dark red skin and deep red flesh
Sweet, juicy fruit
Ripens mid season
At most partially self-fertile, crops better with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa or Shiro

sources.name: Gardening Know How: How To Grow Satsuma Plums

paragraphs: The Sugar prune is a European plum (Prunus domestica) introduced by Luther Burbank in 1899, raised as a seedling of the French (Agen) prune. It quickly took on commercial importance in the Californian prune districts in the early 1900s.

The fruit is medium to large, larger than the French prune, oval and slightly flattened, with dark reddish purple skin under a thick white bloom. It is freestone, with golden yellow flesh that is juicy, tender and sweet with high sugar content. Despite its name, it tended to dry into a coarse, stringy product, so it was shipped extensively as a fresh plum and used for canning rather than as a top-quality dried prune.

claims.text: European plum (Prunus domestica) introduced by Luther Burbank in 1899 as a seedling of the French (Agen) prune
Took on commercial importance in Californian prune districts from 1903
Medium to large, oval and slightly flattened, with dark reddish purple skin and a thick white bloom; freestone
Golden yellow flesh that is juicy, tender and sweet with high sugar content
Intended as a prune but dries coarse and stringy, so shipped mainly as a fresh plum and used for canning

sources.name: HortScience (ASHS): Luther Burbank's Plums, Vol. 50 Issue 2 (2015)

paragraphs: Victoria is the famous English plum (Prunus domestica), found as a seedling at Alderton, England, and introduced by the nurseryman Denyer in the 1840s as Denyer's Victoria. It went on to become the most popular plum variety in the UK.

The fruit has pale red, lightly speckled skin over golden-yellow flesh, and it is unusually versatile, good both fresh and cooked, where it makes excellent jam and pie fillings. Victoria is reliably self-fertile, so it crops without a pollinator, and ripens in late summer to early autumn.

claims.text: English plum (Prunus domestica) found at Alderton, England, and introduced by the nurseryman Denyer in the 1840s as Denyer's Victoria
Pale red, lightly speckled skin over golden-yellow flesh
Versatile for both dessert and culinary use, including jam and pie fillings
Reliably self-fertile, so it crops without a pollinator
Ripens in late summer to early autumn (August to September)

sources.name: Brickfields Park: Victoria Plum (Prunus domestica Victoria)
